content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You know the exact moment. You are mid-thought, the answer is finally getting good, and the free AI stops cold: “You have reached your limit. Upgrade to continue.”
“Free” almost never means free. It usually means free until you actually start using it, and then a wall.
So I spent a week doing something petty. I sat down with the popular free AI chatbots and used them until they stopped me. No tricks, no scripts, just a steady stream of the kind of questions a normal person asks in a busy afternoon. I wanted to know which ones genuinely let you keep going, and which ones are a free trial wearing a “free” badge.
Here is what actually happened.
How I tested
Two things I cared about:

• The wall. How many real messages before it slows down, quietly drops you to a weaker model, or asks for money.

• The gate. Whether I had to create an account, hand over an email, or “verify” before the first question.
I did not measure who is smartest. That is a different article, and honestly the answer changes every month. I measured who lets you keep asking.
The results
ChatGPT (free, no login). You can now use it without an account, which is genuinely nice. But the good model is on a short leash: a handful of messages and you are quietly moved to the smaller one, and lean on it long enough and you hit a cooldown. Free, but rationed.
Google Gemini (free). Usable without much fuss, and fine for everyday questions. The best model and the higher limits keep nudging you toward an account, and long sessions slow down.
Claude (free). Lovely to talk to, and the tightest cap of the bunch. One real conversation and you are done for a few hours, waiting for the limit to reset. Great for a focused question, frustrating for a long afternoon.
Perplexity (free). A different animal. It is an answer engine with sources, and the free version is generous for quick look-ups. The deeper “Pro” search is counted, but for “what is the deal with X,” free is plenty.
DuckDuckGo AI Chat (duck.ai). The privacy pick. No account, no email, and it forgets the conversation on purpose. You get a choice of a few smaller models. It is lean by design, not a powerhouse, but it genuinely does not make you sign up.
HuggingChat. Free and open, running community models. The catch is you need a Hugging Face account to use it, which for this test counts as a gate.
GetAskAI. The one that did not wall me. No account, no email, you open getaskai.com and start typing, and across a long messy session I never hit a message counter. It also did the two things I keep wanting from a quick tool: when I asked about something recent it searched the web and showed the sources, and it read a PDF I threw at it. It is not pretending to be a frontier model, it routes your question to solid free models, but for “just answer me and do not stop,” it was the smoothest of the lot.
So which ones actually have no limit
Let me be honest about the phrase “no limit.” Nothing is infinite. Every one of these has some abuse protection in the background, because inference costs real money and someone always tries to script it. But from the seat of a normal human asking normal questions, only two never made me stop or sign up:

• GetAskAI — no account and no message cap I could find, plus web search and file reading.

• DuckDuckGo AI Chat — no account, private by design, if you are fine with smaller models.
Perplexity earns an honorable mention for look-ups. The big three (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude) are excellent, and also the quickest to put a wall or a login in front of you.
The catch nobody mentions
“No limit” and “no account” pull against the business. An account is how a service rations cost and remembers you. Remove it and the company gives up its retention hook and takes on a bill that grows with every stranger. That is exactly why most free tiers cap you: the cap is the business model, dressed up as a feature.
Which is the quiet reason to appreciate the tools that do not do it. When something is genuinely free and open, it is usually paying for itself another way, ads most often, and that only works if the tool is actually good enough that people come back. It is a cleaner deal than a countdown.
Bottom line
If your problem is that free AI keeps stopping you, stop starting with the big names and try a no-account one first. I would open GetAskAI for a long working session, keep DuckDuckGo’s chat for anything private, and reach for Perplexity when I need sources. Save your ChatGPT and Claude messages for the questions that really need the smartest model, since those are the ones that run out.
The best free AI is the one still answering when you are on your tenth question. Test it yourself, that is the only benchmark that matters.
